---
title: "AI Tools for Data Science and Statistics"
subtitle: "Session 3: PHI, Privacy, and Simulated-Data Workflows"
author: "Erik Westlund, PhD"
date: "June 17, 2026"
bibliography: temp_source/references.bib
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    embed-resources: true
    incremental: true
    css: temp_source/slides.css
---

## Goals For Session 3

- Explain why PHI changes AI-assisted workflow design
- Separate code movement from data movement
- Use simulated data for development and review
- Identify what information can safely leave a restricted environment only after project-specific approval
- Prepare final-project workflows without exposing restricted data to AI tools

## Core Principle

Code can move across computing environments; sensitive data cannot.

## What Counts As Sensitive Here?

- Direct identifiers
- Dates, geography, rare conditions, and small cells that can become identifying
- Free text
- Linkable combinations of variables
- Restricted-use datasets even when they do not look individually identifying
- Project-specific metadata covered by agreements or policy

## Agent Risk Surface

- A chat tool sees what you paste or upload.
- An agent can inspect files it has permission to read.
- If sensitive files are in the workspace, assume the agent may access them.
- Policy and data-use agreements determine what is allowed.
- Vendor terms do not override institutional or legal restrictions.

## Safe Workflow Shape

1. Work with restricted data only in the approved environment.
2. Extract only approved structural information.
3. Develop code outside the restricted environment using simulated data.
4. Review generated code before it returns to the restricted environment.
5. Run reviewed code on real data only where real data are allowed.
6. Export only approved aggregate outputs.

## Structural Information

Structural information may include, when allowed:

- Variable names
- Variable types
- Value labels
- Allowed categories
- Plausible ranges
- Missing-value codes
- Broad missingness patterns
- Non-disclosive row and column counts

## Information To Treat Carefully

- Exact rare category counts
- Extreme values
- Dates and timestamps
- Geographic detail
- Free-text examples
- Unusual variable combinations
- Any output that could identify a person, clinic, provider, school, or small group

## Simulated Data Purpose

Simulated data are useful for:

- Building cleaning code
- Testing joins and reshaping
- Prototyping tables and plots
- Debugging model syntax
- Reviewing workflow structure

Simulated data are not final evidence.

## No External Public Repository Requirement

This workflow does not require an external public repository.

Code can move through institution-approved mechanisms such as:

- approved internal repositories
- secure file transfer
- controlled shared drives
- manual code transfer reviewed before use

The key rule is that sensitive data do not leave the approved environment.

## Final Project Connection

- Develop on simulated or public data.
- Keep real or restricted data out of AI-visible workspaces.
- Submit a workflow that shows what was developed with AI assistance.
- Include a reflection on what was checked manually.
- Do not include restricted data in submitted materials.

## Wrap-Up

- Privacy is a workflow constraint, not an afterthought.
- Agents increase the importance of file boundaries and permissions.
- Simulated data support development, not final inference.
- The analyst remains responsible for correctness, privacy, and interpretation.
